ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general French toast entry; kaya-spread variant is a Singaporean adaptation not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for French toast with kaya. A00GJ (bread-based prepared dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean bread-based dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of French toast with kaya in Singapore consists of two slices of egg-coated pan-fried bread spread with kaya, weighing approximately 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: French Toast with Kaya — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for French toast with kaya (cr_id F05020045). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from HPB compositional data for kaya and frying oil components; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, vitamins, minerals beyond those reported, and moisture are not separately reported in the HPB source and are set to -1 as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White bread analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean French toast with kaya in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated using white bread as the primary analogous food (GI ~71–75, University of Sydney GI Database). The egg coating and frying process do not substantially lower GI relative to the white bread base. Kaya adds concentrated sugar with negligible fibre, maintaining a high GI profile. GL per serving calculated as: GI (72) × available carbohydrate per serving (30.9 g from 120 g serving) / 100 = 22.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (308 mg/100 g) combined with negligible potassium (6 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2.15 g/100 g), producing an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid French toast with kaya due to its high sodium content of 308 mg/100 g, extremely low potassium of 6 mg/100 g, and added sugar from kaya that further elevates cardiovascular risk; if consumed, portion should be restricted to one slice and paired with potassium-rich foods such as banana or low-fat yogurt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from refined white bread base, high sugar content (9.27 g/100 g) from kaya, and low dietary fibre (2.15 g/100 g) resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: French toast with kaya is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~72), high sugar load from kaya (9.27 g/100 g), and low fibre (2.15 g/100 g); if consumed, substituting wholemeal bread, requesting minimal kaya, and pairing with a protein source can partially attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~3.8 g/100 g from coconut milk in kaya and frying oil, combined with high sodium (308 mg/100 g) and high sugar (9.27 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume French toast with kaya sparingly, as the estimated saturated fat of approximately 3.8 g/100 g from coconut-milk-based kaya and frying oil, combined with 308 mg/100 g sodium and 9.27 g/100 g sugar, collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; limiting portion to one slice and requesting reduced kaya is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: French toast with kaya is a popular Singaporean breakfast dish consisting of thick-cut white bread slices dipped in beaten egg and pan-fried or grilled until golden brown, then spread generously with kaya — a sweet, fragrant coconut jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaves. The dish is a staple of traditional Singaporean kopitiam (coffee shop) culture and is commonly served alongside soft-boiled eggs and kopi (local coffee). The texture is crisp on the outside with a soft, eggy interior, and the kaya imparts a rich, sweet, coconut-pandan flavour. Per 100 g, the dish provides 234 kcal, 10.36 g protein, 9.95 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~3.8 g from coconut milk and frying oil), and 25.75 g carbohydrate with 9.27 g sugar and 2.15 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 308 mg/100 g and potassium is very low at 6 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without modification. The dish is vegetarian and halal but not vegan due to egg content. Clinically, the combination of high GI, high sugar, high sodium, and low potassium and fibre makes this a high-concern food for diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for French toast with kaya (cr_id F05020045) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest defensible approved-source match. Saturated fat is estimated from known compositional data for kaya and frying oil components as the HPB source does not separately report fatty acid fractions.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) for this specific Singaporean French toast with kaya preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026; no alternative approved source provides these values for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: full vitamin panel for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026; no alternative approved source provides these values for this specific preparation. phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: extended mineral panel beyond potassium, calcium, and iron is not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this dish as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026. All -1 values confirmed unavailable in HPB Foodo (primary approved source) and USDA FoodData Central (secondary approved source) for this specific Singaporean composite dish. Saturated fat of 3.8 g/100 g is estimated from known kaya composition (coconut milk base) and typical frying oil contribution; this value should be confirmed if a full fatty acid panel becomes available from HPB. The input dietary_tags field contained 'wholegrain' which is inconsistent with the white bread base described in the dish; this tag has been removed as unsupported. Potassium at 6 mg/100 g is unusually low and reflects the HPB source value; this may warrant re-verification against a future updated HPB entry. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate using white bread as the reference food from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources.
